title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Snow crab season closes NASTY: High winds, big waves batter boats, but quota near. By Jim Paulin The Associated Press (Published February 15, 2001) Unalaska -- State Fish and Game biologists announced Wednesday they would close the Bering Sea snow crab regular season just before midnight. The season opened Jan. 15, but nearly all the 200-plus vessels waited to start fishing until Feb. 3, following a strike that ended when fishermen accepted $1.55 per pound from processors in Unalaska, St. Paul, King Cove and Akutan. The season was punctuated by storms, strong winds and tall waves that crashed through windows of five crabbers harvesting shellfish in heavy metal pots. "We've had a lot of really heavy currents, and the big tides and currents coupled with high winds going the other way mak…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published February 20, 2001) U.S. stock and oil markets were closed Monday in recognition of Presidents Day. City of Lights victors announced The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce has selected the winners for the 2000-2001 City of Lights Competition. The competition ran from Oct. 31 through Jan. 31 and was open to people who decorated their houses or businesses with white lights. The houses and businesses were judged on (1) quantity of lights in proportion to size of property (2) creativity and originality and (3) neighborhood enhancement. The winners each receive two tickets to Hawaii, courtesy of Hawaiian Vacations. And the winners are: In Anchorage - Duplex - Erich and Beverly Rusche - 8621 and 8623 Shrub Court (off Muldoon and 20th Avenue). In Eagle River and Fo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Log Ends Snowmachine Dreams Evan Booth breaks tibia, dislocates hip in accident By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published February 20, 2001) One moment, two-time Iron Dog snowmobile race champion Evan Booth was sailing along at 85 mph in the dark, just five miles outside McGrath. The next moment, he was airborne. Booth's snowmachine snagged on a hidden piece of log, sending him an estimated 150 feet in the air. His landing dislocated his right hip and broke his tibia, the thicker of the two bones between the knee and ankle. "That pretty much brought things to an abrupt stop," Booth said from his hospital bed at Alaska Regional Hospital, where he was in traction late Monday night. "I remember every bit of it," said Booth, a 32-year-old from Nome. "Flying through the air, cartw…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Firm backs highway route for gas ANALYSIS A rival pipeline proposal is riddled with problems, two experts say. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published February 20, 2001) The owners of critical permits to build a natural gas pipeline along the Alaska Highway to the Lower 48 said Monday that the highway route offers the fastest and possibly the cheapest way to get the state's vast, undeveloped natural gas reserves to market. "That may not be what you expected to hear," John Ellwood, a vice president of Calgary-based Foothills Pipeline, said after a presentation to the Senate Resources Committee in Juneau. Under the Foothills assessment, a 1,700-mile pipeline from Prudhoe Bay to Fairbanks, then down the Alaska Highway to the pipeline grid in Canada carrying 4 billion cubic feet of g…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Native venture is a big winner WIRELESS: Corporate allies win federal airwave licenses in 43 markets. By Kalpana Srinivasan (Published January 27, 2001) A venture involving three Alaska Native corporations was a big winner as wireless companies shelled out a record $16.86 billion for federal airwaves licenses. The licenses could mean better service, greater coverage and expanded services for consumers, including phone text messages and high-speed Internet access. Alaska Native Wireless, a venture involving Arctic Slope Regional Corp. of Barrow, Sealaska Corp. of Juneau and Doyon Ltd. of Fairbanks, won licenses in 43 markets with $2.9 billion in bids, the company said. The consortium also includes AT&T Wireless, which contributed $2.6 billion in capital for the bids, while the Na…

title: SMALL CARIBOU HERDS FACE STRUGGLE TO SURVIVE
summary: While some Alaska caribou herds are growing to immense size, three small ones are declining so rapidly that some biologists think they may go extinct within the decade. Two of the disappearing herds, the Mentasta and Chisana, live mainly inside Wrangell St. Elias National Park and Preserve except during the winter when they migrate toward and sometimes into Canada. The third, the Beaver Mountain herd, roams west of McGrath. The Mentasta herd now totals between 350 and 450, compared to a high of 3,500 in 1987, according to state biologists. The Chisana herd has dropped to about 350, down from about 1,500 in 1989. The Beaver Mountain herd has declined even further, to 125 from around 3,000 animals in the early 1960s, said Pat Valkenburg, a caribou expert and research coordinator with the Ala…

title: Cap would cost 3,500 jobs, study says
summary: A University of Alaska study released Wednesday concluded that the statewide property tax initiative, if approved in November, would initially cost Alaska more than 3,500 jobs and result in 2,220 households leaving the state. Complete election coverage Eventually, about half the job losses would be restored as residents spent their collective $280 million in tax savings, the study said. But there's no quick fix to replace the revenue lost to local governments by the reduction in property taxes, unless Anchorage and Fairbanks residents agree to sales taxes, the Permanent Fund is tapped, a state income tax is reinstated or tourists can be somehow forced to pay. The study, by economist Scott Goldsmith and analyst Alexandra Hill of the Institute for Social and Economic Research, said tax cap s…

title: Agency seeks 4 more years for grad exam
summary: State Board of Education members say they'd like to put off the consequences of new state tests for high school graduation for four years and, in the meantime, solve some complex problems that have arisen since the first tests were given last March. "The simple concept of a test seemed like a good idea," said member Paula Pawlowski. "I just don't think we're ready for it." In meetings in Anchorage Thursday and Friday, board members raised these concerns: Military students will come to Alaska late in their high school careers and be denied diplomas. Students from poor areas of Alaska, and those who speak Yup'ik or anything other than English as their first language, won't have enough time to master requirements of the new tests. Children with disabilities won't have a fair chance with just …

title: Doing time means doing homework for teen inmates at Spring Creek High
summary: Seward -- Jeremy McAnulty once shot a man, then beat him so hard with the rifle that it snapped in two. Now he's studying American literature. Inmate Jeremy McAnulty and teacher Alice Tucker discuss a lesson. Tucker says there are "some bright, bright kids here." (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) The page of his textbook a few days ago was opened to one of Langston Hughes' most famous poems, "The Negro Speaks of Rivers." Short and earthy, the poem braids the passage of time with descriptions of powerful rivers: "I heard the singing of the Mississippi when Abe Lincoln went down to New Orleans, and I've seen its muddy bosom turn all golden in the sunset." "He's talking about his soul," McAnulty said. "The person he's talking about is a strong person." "I've always dug poetry, man," he sa…

title: KENAI BIDS FOR PRISON
summary: Soldotna -- With the fate of Fort Greely tied in knots, the Kenai Peninsula Borough has offered the state a location for its much-needed medium-security prison. It suggests building a new 1,000-bed facility near the city of Kenai. The borough's proposal, made July 25, got a lukewarm response Tuesday from Department of Corrections Commissioner Margaret Pugh, but she didn't dismiss the idea out of hand. Pugh said Fort Greely is still the state's first choice, but she conceded that the ongoing debate over a national missile defense system has clouded its future. The Army post near Delta Junction could become a missile base if the defense system is approved. When or if that might happen is anybody's guess; critics say it won't work and are trying to derail the proposal. The state, meanwhile, w…

title: New rule hands some illegal immigrants 4 months to become legal
summary: Like all immigrants with status problems, Sergio Vazquez walked a razor's edge. He worked hard and lived a respectable life, but he was "illegal" and had to worry all the time about getting caught. Sergio Vazquez will be able to apply for a green card without having to leave his wife, Tammie, and their son, Sergio Jr. "He was all happy when we got the news," she says. "It's a big present for me, too." (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Vazquez once spent two weeks in jail after police did a routine ID check and discovered he didn't have permission to be in the United States. Now he hopes all that is finished. A stroke of President Clinton's pen this week guaranteed a merrier Christmas for Vazquez and hundreds of other Alaska immigrants facing deportation and, in some cases, separation f…

title: CARCASSES SEEN AS VITAL TO RIVERS
summary: Juneau -- Those stinky dead salmon bobbing along the edges of countless Alaska rivers and streams lately appear to hold the key to producing vibrant and abundant young fish stocks, according to a study by a group of scientists here. And the number of decaying carcasses needed to create the ideal, nutrient-rich stream may be much greater than state fish managers are allowing to slip away from commercial fisheries to return to spawn, the research suggests. Whether in salmon-rich waters in Alaska or in the nutrient-starved streams in the Pacific Northwest, "this has major implications for salmon management," said Mark Wipfli, who is heading up the salmon carcass study for the U.S. Forest Service's Pacific Northwest Research Station in Juneau. It poses new questions, he said, for officials who…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | CLINTON STANDS FIRM ON ANWR DRILLING By Anne Gearan The Associated Press (Published September 15, 2000) Washington -- The Clinton administration said Thursday that rising fuel prices do not justify new oil drilling in environmentally sensitive parts of the United States. The Clinton administration has long opposed new exploration in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge and in several other protected areas, arguing that the potential for harm to the environment far outweighs possible gains in oil production. "The president, at his core, does not believe that the right approach here is to start drilling in Alaska or in the protected areas," White House spokesman Joe Lockhart said. "There is no consideration of that," he added. The price of oil is near a 10-year high, affecting gasoline an…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | WILDLIFE NUMBERS DON'T ADD UP TO INEPT By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published September 17, 2000) CROW CREEK ROAD -- On the gravel coming up from the Crow Creek Mine, an older Chevrolet Blazer, the full-size model, rolled to a halt. Inside sat two young men in fatigues. I was on a trail coming uphill from the direction of Glacier Creek. "Is the creek down there?" the driver asked. "Yeah," I said. "Why?" "We're thinking about going hunting," he said. "What for?" "Moose and bear." A browsing bull moose. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Standing there beside the door in running shorts and a sweaty singlet, I wanted to laugh. "You got any idea how many moose there are in the Girdwood Valley?" I asked. "No," the driver said. I held up two fingers. This might have been a low…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | SMALL CARIBOU HERDS FACE STRUGGLE TO SURVIVE By Elizabeth Manning Daily News Reporter (Published September 12, 2000) While some Alaska caribou herds are growing to immense size, three small ones are declining so rapidly that some biologists think they may go extinct within the decade. Two of the disappearing herds, the Mentasta and Chisana, live mainly inside Wrangell St. Elias National Park and Preserve except during the winter when they migrate toward and sometimes into Canada. The third, the Beaver Mountain herd, roams west of McGrath. The Mentasta herd now totals between 350 and 450, compared to a high of 3,500 in 1987, according to state biologists. The Chisana herd has dropped to about 350, down from about 1,500 in 1989. The Beaver Mountain herd has declined even further, to 125 …

title: LATE DEAL KEEPS YOUNG BILL ALIVE
summary: Washington -- Agreement was reached Thursday night on an Interior Department spending bill that would earmark $12 billion in revenues from offshore oil drilling for land acquisition and conservation programs. "This is the largest increase in conservation spending in the history of the country," said Washington Rep. Norm Dicks, the senior Democrat on the House Interior Appropriations Committee, who brokered the deal. But it's unclear whether the money and the programs will be sufficient to appease a broad coalition of conservation, wildlife, civic and recreation groups organized behind a much more sweeping program. That coalition of more than 5,000 groups backed legislation sponsored by Alaska Rep. Don Young and Sen. Frank Murkowski, called the Conservation and Reinvestment Act. The CARA le…

title: WILD WHALE CHASE: BELUGAS HARD TO CATCH
summary: Knik Arm -- As three big beluga whales swam from the mouth of Eagle River on the falling tide, a team of federal scientists and whale experts in four boats raced across the swirling waters of Knik Arm to cut them off before deep water. National Oceanic And Atmospheric Administration Officer Bob Yerena, left, and National Marine Fisheries biologist Dan Vos examine a baby beluga found dead at Mile 84 of the Seward Highway on Sunday. The biologist found no signs of trauma and removed it to have a necropsy performed. Scientists have been trying to catch and tag mature belugas but do not track cows and calves. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) The scientists' goal was to capture a beluga for a satellite transmitter, joining two other whales fitted with tracking tags the day before. Uncoveri…

title: 'DIE,' BIOLOGIST SAYS AS PESTICIDE CLEARS PERCH FROM LAKE
summary: Nikiski -- State fish biologist Mike Bethe left little doubt about his feelings Tuesday morning as he glared at the frenzied splashing of thousands of yellow perch suffocating in the poisoned waters of a small lake. "Die," Bethe said. A crew of three in a state Department of Fish and Game skiff had spent a couple of hours spraying about 650 pounds of the pesticide rotenone into the unnamed, 11-acre lake. From the shore, Bethe, about a dozen other state sportfish biologists and two observers from the state Department of Environmental Conservation watched as the four-inch perch first started thrashing, then washing up on shore dead. The biologists were all curious about how rotenone, a common pesticide, worked. It is said to pose little risk to humans and other mammals, and state officials w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | The gift of time New rule hands some illegal immigrants 4 months to become legal By Sheila Toomey Daily News Reporter (Published December 24, 2000) Like all immigrants with status problems, Sergio Vazquez walked a razor's edge. He worked hard and lived a respectable life, but he was "illegal" and had to worry all the time about getting caught. Sergio Vazquez will be able to apply for a green card without having to leave his wife, Tammie, and their son, Sergio Jr. "He was all happy when we got the news," she says. "It's a big present for me, too." (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Vazquez once spent two weeks in jail after police did a routine ID check and discovered he didn't have permission to be in the United States. Now he hopes all that is finished. A stroke of President Clinto…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | KENAI BIDS FOR PRISON PLAN AN ALTERNATIVE TO FORT GREELY SITE By Jon Little Daily News Peninsula Bureau (Published August 2, 2000) Soldotna -- With the fate of Fort Greely tied in knots, the Kenai Peninsula Borough has offered the state a location for its much-needed medium-security prison. It suggests building a new 1,000-bed facility near the city of Kenai. The borough's proposal, made July 25, got a lukewarm response Tuesday from Department of Corrections Commissioner Margaret Pugh, but she didn't dismiss the idea out of hand. Pugh said Fort Greely is still the state's first choice, but she conceded that the ongoing debate over a national missile defense system has clouded its future. The Army post near Delta Junction could become a missile base if the defense system is approved. Wh…

title: Battle to save sea lion threatens vital state lifeline
summary: For Anchorage residents who haven't been much beyond Palmer or the Russian River on the Kenai, Alaska's coastal fishing communities may as well be on the moon. But people in those towns are pretty jittery these days, and there's some good reasons the rest of Alaska should care. Putting it simply, our offshore fisheries may soon become managed by the courts instead of biologists and scientists. Communities from Kodiak to Unalaska may become like those Pacific Northwest timber towns that lost their base industry in the wake of the spotted-owl lawsuits. Remember that? It was also an Endangered Species Act lawsuit, just like this lawsuit case in Alaska brought by Greenpeace over Steller sea lions. And guess what. The same judge is in charge in Seattle U.S. District Court. Here's the latest: St…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | LATE DEAL KEEPS YOUNG BILL ALIVE ALASKANS DENOUNCE DEMOCRAT'S TRIMS IN CONSERVATION SPENDING PROPOSAL By David Whitney Daily News Washington Bureau (Published September 29, 2000) Washington -- Agreement was reached Thursday night on an Interior Department spending bill that would earmark $12 billion in revenues from offshore oil drilling for land acquisition and conservation programs. "This is the largest increase in conservation spending in the history of the country," said Washington Rep. Norm Dicks, the senior Democrat on the House Interior Appropriations Committee, who brokered the deal. But it's unclear whether the money and the programs will be sufficient to appease a broad coalition of conservation, wildlife, civic and recreation groups organized behind a much more sweeping prog…

title: BRISTOL BAY REPORTS BOOST IN PROFIT TO $11.2 MILLION
summary: Bristol Bay Native Corp. reported a fiscal 2000 profit of $11.2 million, up from $4.6 million a year earlier. Revenue for the 12 months ended March 31 was $132 million, up from $88.1 million a year earlier, and $8.7 million in 1997. The largest source of profits came from the company's $100 million in stocks and bonds, which represent about 70 percent of corporate assets. That portfolio returned $16.2 million in profits, according to Bristol Bay's annual report. The company's auditor, Deloitte & Touche, noted that Bristol Bay changed its accounting method in 1997 so that unrealized gains and losses from stock and bond investments would show on the profit statement. Normally, not all stocks and bonds would be classified that way, and if generally accepted practices were followed, the compan…

title: NORTH POLE IS MELTING -- WELL, IT'S MORE COMPLICATED
summary: "The North Pole is melting" declared the lead story in The New York Times two weeks ago. In a story that surprised readers across the nation, the Times reported that "an ice-free patch of ocean about a mile wide has opened at the very top of the world, something that has presumably never before been seen by humans. . . . "The last time scientists can be certain the pole was awash in water was more than 50 million years ago." The paper characterized the observation, made by scientists and tourists on a July trip to the Pole aboard a Russian icebreaker, as evidence of global warming's relentless acceleration in the Arctic. Not exactly, the paper admitted a week later. Open water at the North Pole isn't that unusual. And the condition of the Arctic Ocean's ice cap and its relationship to glob…

title: CLARK NABS 19TH IN SYDNEY
summary: Another marathon, another magical moment. Anchorage's Chris Clark ran the most important race of her life Saturday afternoon (Alaska time) on nothing less than the biggest stage in sports and responded with yet another remarkable performance. The 37-year-old pathologist slashed another two minutes off her personal-best time, covering a hilly course in 2 hours, 31 minutes, 35 seconds to finish 19th in the Olympic Games in Sydney, Australia. U.S. runner Chris Clark rests after finishing 19th in the women's marathon final at the Summer Olympics Sunday (Saturday in Alaska) at Olympic Stadium in Sydney, Australia. (AP Photo / Doug Mills) For the up-to-the-minute news and results from Sydney, go to adn.com's Olympics site . ''I am so relieved,'' Clark said by cell phone from Olympic Park in Sydn…

Revenue for the 12 months ended March 31 was $132 million, up from $88.1 million a year earlier, and $8.7 million in 1997. The company's auditor, Deloitte & Touche, noted that Bristol Bay changed its accounting method in 1997 so that unrealized gains and losses from stock and bond investments would show on the profit statement. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | NORTH POLE IS MELTING -- WELL, IT'S MORE COMPLICATED By Doug O'harra Daily News Reporter (Published September 4, 2000) "The North Pole is melting" declared the lead story in The New York Times two weeks ago. In a story that surprised readers across the nation, the Times reported that "an ice-free patch of ocean about a mile wide has opened at the very top of the world, something that has presumably never before been seen by humans. . . . "The last time scientists can be certain the pole was awash in water was more than 50 million years ago." The paper characterized the observation, made by scientists and tourists on a July trip to the Pole aboard a Russian icebreaker, as evidence of global warming's relentless acceleration in the Arctic. Not exactly, the paper admitted a week later. Op…

title: Alaska voters spurn marijuana initiative
summary: Marijuana proponents took a hard hit from voters who rejected the proposition that not only would have legalized the drug for adults, but would have opened jail doors and urged restitution for marijuana offenders. Click here for latest vote totals With three-quarters of the precincts counted, the measure was failing by a nearly 3-to-2 ratio, and supporters conceded defeat. "We knew it'd be uphill," said Al Anders, chairman of Free Hemp in Alaska. "At least we got the chance to educate voters that the Ravin decision is still the law, and all these small marijuana busts are unconstitutional." The Ravin decision was a 1975 Alaska Supreme Court opinion that found marijuana so innocuous that the government had no business pursuing adults for possessing limited amounts in private. Some voters, l…

title: VOTERS GET CHANCE TO AGAIN BAN LAND-AND-SHOOT WOLF HUNTING
summary: The question of how hard it should be to kill wolves is heading once again for the state ballot, adding another turn in this passionate debate. The Division of Elections on Tuesday said a group opposed to land-and-shoot wolf hunting raised enough signatures, more than the required 22,716, to put a referendum before voters in the Nov. 7 election. The land-and-shoot issue has had a couple of turns since 1996. That year, Alaskans voted to ban hunters from shooting wolves on the same day they flew to a hunting area. This spring, the Alaska Legislature largely overturned the ban by passing a Senate bill that allows land-and-shoot hunting in areas designated by state Board of Game. The referendum, which will appear as ballot measure 6 in November, will overturn that Senate bill, in essence keepi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoor Hotline Compiled By By Craig Medred Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published January 30, 2001) WILDLIFE Goat with troubling disease seen; hunters cautioned An Alaska Department of Fish and Game biologist reports a goat was observed and photographed at Horn Cliff near Petersburg with severe contagious ecthyma. The goat has severe lesions around the nose, mouth, eyes and ears, according to the report. While there have been sporadic reports of infected goats throughout Game Management Unit 1 on the Southeast mainland in recent years, this is the first documented case. Biologists consider this viral outbreak serious. Because the disease is transmissible to humans via contact with the lesions, hunters and biologists are being cautioned to wear rubber gloves when handling ani…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hunting permit winners State draws sheep and Koyukuk moose hunting permits earlier (Published January 30, 2001) You are a winner of an Alaska sheep or moose hunting permit, maybe. At least the odds were better this time around. A significant number of hunters apparently missed the shift in deadlines from spring to winter for some permit hunts, according to the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. The state agency moved the application deadline for Koyukuk moose hunts and all Dall sheep hunts to get permits to hunters earlier, thus providing them time to prepare for late summer hunts. Instead of applying for a Chugach sheep hunting permit before Labor Day, for instance, and waiting until only a couple weeks before the sheep season to find out whether such a hunt would be possible, hunter…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published January 30, 2001) EDUCATION Class on tracking offered Space remains in this winter's "Nature Observation and Tracking" class, according to the University of Alaska Anchorage. The three-credit course covers the basics of tracking and observing Alaska mammals. It includes classroom study and five, one-day field outings to find wildlife. Students taking the class should be able to function comfortably in bad weather. Classes start Monday. Cost is $467; pre-registration is required. For more, call the UAA outdoor program at 786-4066. 13-lesson boating course is set "Boating Skills and Seamanship," a 13-lesson course for recreational boaters, starts Monday at Gruening Middle School, according to Doris Thomas, spokeswoman for Flotilla 2-3 of the U.S. Coast Guard Auxili…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Watch your step with alternative footwear for ice OPTIONS: Stabilicers and Yaktrax can help on slick treks By Craig Medred (Published January 30, 2001) What might be the perfect footwear for Anchorage trails and paths circa 2001 was invented long ago: the ice skate. The silver blades were an early technological innovation designed to take advantage of slip-sliding around on ice. Some of the skaters seen on area bike trails in recent weeks obviously understand this. But the reality appears to be that either few Anchorage residents skate or aren't willing to venture onto the ice-snow-gravel-dirt-pavement mix found on local bike and ski trails in expensive blades. Let's face it, while the trails can be rink-like at times, they usually have debris, too. Gravel, in particular, can easily da…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | All-weather Runner Snow and ice aren't good enough reasons to stay in this winter By Shawn Lyons (Published January 30, 2001) Wind, rain, snow, sleet, ice, you name, it, I run every day, year round. The only time I don't run is when I'm injured or hiking. Of course, I usually run my hikes if they're short. So, really, about the only time I don't run is if I'm injured. The step machine or stair climber at the gym is usually better for avoiding aggravating the injury. In most places in the Lower 48, running year round is no big deal. Sure winters are snowy in upstate New York and Minnesota, cold and windy in the Dakotas, and wet in Oregon and Washington. But in those places winter is one of four seasons, and, at worst, conditions pass in three months or less. Not so in Alaska. Here winte…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Do you have a proper license for that recreation? By Craid Medred (Published January 30, 2001) Olympian Nina Kemppel never said a word. She didn't have to. The look said it all. The look said, "C'mon, man, use your head." Or, at least that would be the polite interpretation. Kemppel was working on her classical ski technique on the trails above the Hatcher Pass Lodge. I was on skate skis. Skate skiing on the trails between the lodge and the closed-for-the-winter Independence Mine State Historical Park was lousy, but I hadn't known that when I clicked into the skis at the parking lot. The road from the parking lot to the mine, unmaintained in winter, made for good skating. It was packed solid 10-feet wide. So I chugged past dog-walkers, hikers and parents pulling children in sleds, anti…

title: It may be ugly, but it may save your skull
summary: Maybe my big brother Will was a little tired. Maybe the skimpy conditions -- 3 inches of powder on top of bulletproof snow -- played a part. Maybe he was going too hard too early in the season. Who knows? But after hitting four turns high on a slope at Alta, Utah, he wiped and began a headfirst, downhill slide. When he stopped, we could see blood oozing through his ski hat. The tail of his ski clipped his head. "Doesn't hurt," he said. "Does it look bad?" Only like a 3-inch gash at the top of his skull. Will's cut wasn't bad as far head injuries go. He never blacked out. He had no brain damage, as far as I could tell. After 12 stitches and some Advil, he was fine. Opposed to helmets before, he's now considering a brain bucket. Still he could have been a Sonny Bono, a Michael Kennedy. Every…

title: Murkowski promotes gas line, rail link to Canada
summary: Fairbanks -- Sen. Frank Murkowski is back at home in Alaska, where he's touting the construction of a trans-Alaska natural gas pipeline. A gas line also could help spur development of a railroad linking Alaska to Canada and the Lower 48, Murkowski said. The Alaska Republican made the comments Monday while talking with reporters and speaking with students and faculty at the University of Alaska Fairbanks. Murkowski also addressed opening the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling. If the North Slope's natural gas deposits are commercialized, then petrochemicals for such uses as plastics could be developed from gas liquids in Alaska and shipped on the railroad, Murkowski said. Fairbanks could be the location for that kind of petrochemical plant, he said. "That p…

title: Salmon count fluctuated with climate, study says
summary: Alaska salmon runs appear to flourish or struggle in direct relation to climatic warming and cooling, according to a group of scientists who managed to trace some of the state's red salmon returns back 300 years. The findings, published in the journal Science, could have broad implications for future management, said lead researcher Bruce Finney of the Institute of Marine Science at the University of Alaska Fairbanks. Finney is trying to track salmon returns thousands of years back to a medieval warm period when the Vikings flourished in Greenland. The strength of Alaska salmon runs then could indicate what might happen if global warming continues. Research indicates Alaska salmon do best in periods of relatively high temperatures and fade in cooler periods, Finney said. But none of the wa…

title: Adventure-racing boom gives Iditasport big boost
summary: The human-powered little brother to the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race is bulking up its wallet and its field. "I kind of boasted of a million-dollar purse in five years," Dan Bull, Iditasport director. Almost 200 people are expected to point mountain bikes, skis, snowshoes or running shoes north from Big Lake in February to chase $80,000 in prize money up the Iditarod Trail to Finger Lake, McGrath and Nome. There are three different Iditasport races. The shortest is from Big Lake to Finger Lake. The longest goes all the way to Nome. Last year, the purse was $2,500, and the entry fee was $500. More than 100 racers, including a large contingent of Europeans, have already paid entry fees, Iditasport director Dan Bull said Monday. "People are coming from all over the friggin' world," he said. "…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | INS' reversal gives Iranian his U.S. citizenship By Lisa Demer Daily News Reporter (Published October 21, 2000) He's a top manager at one of the biggest seafood plants in North America. He loves the Mariners but wears a Yankees cap because it looks so cool. And Friday, David Abbasian did what he's dreamed of but feared would never happen. He became a U.S. citizen. It was a dramatic turn of events for a man who until a few weeks ago faced being deported to his home country of Iran because of an old drug conviction. His attorney said the possibility was strong that Abbasian, who has lived in America more than 20 years, would have been executed in Iran for his Baha'i faith. David Abbasian sits with 36 other people who await the start of the naturalization ceremony Friday at the U.S. Dis…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Area M report suggests change DISASTER But it doesn't endorse specific solutions for reviving the chum runs. By Wesley Loy (Published January 30, 2001) A highly anticipated Board of Fisheries committee report on whether to cut back the False Pass commercial salmon fishery to revive crashing Western Alaska chum runs endorses no major changes. That comes as a relief to False Pass backers, who have been fighting through a nearly monthlong board meeting in Anchorage to save their fishery and stave off what they regard as a threat to Aleut fishing towns along the Alaska Peninsula. But the battle is far from over. The seven-member board will begin deliberations today toward a final vote later this week. And the committee report suggests the False Pass fishery might need to change, although…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Watershed Council says removing dams would be big boon to salmon runs By Elizabeth Manning (Published January 30, 2001) Take the dams out, and the lower reaches of Ship Creek could boast 12 miles of free-flowing water for wild, healthy fish runs. "Ship Creek Unplugged" is how the Anchorage Waterways Council has billed the idea of removing three lower dams that obstruct migrating salmon and alter the character of the stream. Lance Trasky of the Habitat and Restoration Division of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game stands near the old Knik Arm Power Plant. "There is money to take out dams like this," he said. (Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) The council for years has made a mission of restoring and protecting the city's creeks. It hopes people will like its idea of dam-free city …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 2 face charges in dancers' case STRIPPING: Russian women were expecting cultural performances. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published January 10, 2001) A Chugiak man and a Russian are in jail today, charged with lying to get seven Russian dancers into Alaska for "cultural" performances that turned out to be stripping naked at a local nightclub. Immigration agents arrested Tony Kennard of Chugiak on Tuesday afternoon on a felony count of aiding and abetting preparation of false visa applications for the seven women, who range in age from 16 to 30. Kennard told the Immigration and Naturalization Service that he brought the women to Alaska to perform Russian folk dances at a series of events in Anchorage, Eagle River and Homer. The day before, agents arrested Viktor Virchenko …

title: Area M report suggests change
summary: A highly anticipated Board of Fisheries committee report on whether to cut back the False Pass commercial salmon fishery to revive crashing Western Alaska chum runs endorses no major changes. That comes as a relief to False Pass backers, who have been fighting through a nearly monthlong board meeting in Anchorage to save their fishery and stave off what they regard as a threat to Aleut fishing towns along the Alaska Peninsula. But the battle is far from over. The seven-member board will begin deliberations today toward a final vote later this week. And the committee report suggests the False Pass fishery might need to change, although it's vague on exactly how. Alaskans have warred for decades over whether False Pass fishing cuts the flow of chum salmon bound for Western Alaska rivers fart…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | High-profile Alaskans jockey for presidential appointments JOBS: Positions in Bush administration draw eager locals. By Liz Ruskin (Published February 1, 2001) George W. Bush has thousands of political appointments to fill, and dozens of Alaskans are longing for a presidential tap upon their shoulders. State Sen. Drue Pearce, for one, wants a seat on the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, and she has at least one big backer in Washington: U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski. "Aside from her public service in the (state) Senate and in the Alaska State House, she has always taken quite an interest and developed quite an expertise in energy issues," said David Garman, Murkowski's chief of staff. About 25 Alaskans are seeking Washington-based jobs in the new administration, but twice that many …
